nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing streams and rivers in hilly areas, typically at elevations up to 300 m; clear water with gravel or sandy substrate

diet.notes: Believed to feed primarily on insects, larvae, and small invertebrates in the wild. In captivity, have been known to accept flake foods, pellets, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, daphnia, and brine shrimp readily.

summary: A large, fast-moving schooling fish from South Asian hill streams, generally considered suitable for beginners who can provide a long tank with ample open swimming space.
careNotes: Reclassified from Danio aequipinnatus to Devario aequipinnatus in 2009. Note: there is widespread trade confusion between this species and Devario malabaricus (Malabar Danio) — many aquarium sources incorrectly apply the name 'Giant Danio' or 'D. aequipinnatus' to D. malabaricus, which is native to the Western Ghats of India and Sri Lanka and is slightly smaller (7–8 cm). True D. aequipinnatus has a broader range extending to Southeast Asia. Keeping in groups of 6 or more tends to reduce semi-aggressive tendencies toward tankmates. These fish are very fast swimmers and tend to need the longest tank footprint possible — a 120 cm minimum is generally advised. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is important as all Devario species are accomplished jumpers. The species has been documented serving well as a 'dither fish' in cichlid tanks, as their bold, constant movement tends to reduce the stress and aggression of territorial cichlids. Fin nipping tendency is generally minor in properly sized groups but has been reported to increase when underschooled or in cramped conditions. Not fussy about water chemistry within the stated ranges.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers with no parental care. Breeding is considered moderate in difficulty — a dedicated spawning tank with a mesh or marbles substrate to protect eggs from being eaten is typically advised. Water temperature in the upper end of the range (around 26°C) and pH at or below 7.0 have been reported to encourage spawning. Eggs generally hatch within 36 hours; fry become free-swimming within a few days and tend to accept infusoria and baby brine shrimp as first foods.

primary.name: Wikipedia — Giant danio
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for a dedicated Giant Danio care page. Wikipedia covers taxonomy, native range, water parameters, size, and care requirements for D. aequipinnatus.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World — Giant Danio
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–24°C vs Wikipedia 22–27°C — averaged to 22–26°C), size (10–15 cm consistent), pH (6.8–7.5 narrower than Wikipedia 6.0–8.0 — used 6.0–7.5 as middle ground), tank volume (55 gal / 208 L). Noted 'monogamous pairing' breeding claim from this source — not supported by other sources and inconsistent with egg-scatter breeding style documented elsewhere; disregarded. Breeding difficulty listed as 'easy' here vs 'moderate' on other sources — averaged to moderate.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Devario aequipinnatus
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature range 22–25°C, adult size ~15 cm max, schooling requirement of 6+, tank length minimum of 36–48 inches. Notes species is 'not at all fussy about water chemistry.' Confirmed omnivore diet.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Devario malabaricus
additional.notes: Used for cross-reference on genus-level behavior and escape risk (tight-fitting lid essential for all Devario spp.). Note: this page covers D. malabaricus (Malabar Danio), a distinct but frequently confused congener. Escape risk 'high' confirmed for genus. Minimum group size of 8–10 noted (used 6 as the conservative minimum consistent with other sources).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers; dedicated spawning tank with mesh bottom recommended to prevent egg predation by adults. Incubation approximately 24–36 hours.
